target.title: Fix: preserve modelOverride in agent handler (#5369)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the intermittent race condition where sub-agent model overrides were silently ignored (~3% failure rate).

- When `sessions.patch` writes a fresh `modelOverride` between when the agent handler captures its local `entry` reference and when it builds `nextEntryPatch`, the captured stale value would clobber the fresh store value through `mergeSessionEntry({...freshStore, ...patch})`.
- The fix removes the stale `entry?.X` writebacks from `nextEntryPatch` (incl. `modelOverride`, `providerOverride`, `cliSessionIds`, `cliSessionBindings`, `claudeCliSessionId`, gated `pluginOwnerId`). `mergeSessionEntry` already preserves anything not in the patch, so fresh store values survive the write.
- Rotation-clearing for `sessionFile` (added by #79076) is preserved through a conditional spread that only writes `sessionFile: undefined` on session rotation.

## Root Cause

The agent handler was:
1. Reading the session entry early via `loadSessionEntry()` and holding it in a local variable.
2. Building `nextEntryPatch` with `modelOverride: entry?.modelOverride` and several siblings — values read from that captured local entry, not the fresh store.
3. Calling `updateSessionStore()` whose mutator does `mergeSessionEntry(store[primaryKey], nextEntryPatch)` — which spreads `{...freshStore, ...patch}`, so any stale value in the patch clobbers the fresh store value written concurrently (e.g., by `sessions.patch` setting `modelOverride` on a sub-agent between spawn and run).

## Changes

- `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts`: drop stale `entry?.X` writebacks from `nextEntryPatch`. Switch the patch type to `Partial<SessionEntry>`. Gate `pluginOwnerId` behind `entry === undefined`. Convert the `sessionFile` rotation-clearing from #79076 into a conditional spread that fires only when `entry.sessionId !== sessionId`.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`: add the original #5369 regression and a broader regression guard for the whole stale-writeback class (sendPolicy, skillsSnapshot, thinkingLevel, fastMode, verboseLevel, traceLevel, reasoningLevel, systemSent, spawnedWorkspaceDir, spawnDepth, cliSessionIds, cliSessionBindings, claudeCliSessionId).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** When `sessions.patch` writes a fresh `modelOverride` between when the agent handler captures its local `entry` reference and when it builds `nextEntryPatch`, the captured stale value clobbers the fresh store value through `mergeSessionEntry({...freshStore, ...patch})`. Per #5369, this manifested as ~3% of sub-agent runs silently ignoring an explicit model override and falling back to the parent's model.

**Real environment tested:**
- Host: Linux 6.14.0-34-generic on x86_64, Node v22.22.0, openclaw production session-store modules from this branch (`src/config/sessions/store.ts`, `src/config/sessions/types.ts`).
- Multipass guest VM `ai-assistant`: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, IP 10.119.193.216, Node v22.22.2, same modules from this branch via a host mount at `/home/ubuntu/openclaw-pr5369`.

Both run the production `loadSessionStore` / `updateSessionStore` / `mergeSessionEntry` code paths against a real on-disk JSON store in a per-run tmp directory. No mocking. No shims.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
node --import tsx scripts/repro-5369.mts 1000
```

The driver lives in this branch (local-only, intentionally not committed). For each of 1000 iterations it:

1. Initializes a session entry on disk via `updateSessionStore` (no override yet).
2. Captures the entry via `loadSessionStore({ skipCache: true })` — the same shape the agent handler holds in its local `entry` variable.
3. Fires a concurrent `updateSessionStore` to set `modelOverride: haiku-4.5-iter-N` (mimicking what `sessions.patch` does during sub-agent setup).
4. Builds `nextEntryPatch` two ways and applies each through the same `mergeSessionEntry(store[primaryKey], nextEntryPatch)` call shape that `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` uses:
   - PRE-FIX shape: `modelOverride: capturedEntry.modelOverride` (writes back the captured stale `undefined`).
   - POST-FIX shape: `modelOverride` dropped from the patch — `mergeSessionEntry` preserves the fresh store value.
5. Reads the persisted entry back from disk and reports whether the override survived.

**Evidence after fix:**

Host (Linux 6.14, Node v22.22.0):

```
[repro #5369] node v22.22.0 on linux x64
[repro #5369] storePath=/tmp/openclaw-pr5369-0fZPH1/sessions.json
[repro #5369] iterations=1000

[repro #5369] running PRE-FIX patch shape (entry?.modelOverride writeback)...
[repro #5369] PRE-FIX :  1000/1000 sub-agents lost their override  (338 ms)
[repro #5369]           sample: expected=haiku-4.5-iter-0 actual=undefined

[repro #5369] running POST-FIX patch shape (modelOverride dropped from patch)...
[repro #5369] POST-FIX:  0/1000 sub-agents lost their override  (254 ms)
[repro #5369]           sample: expected=haiku-4.5-iter-0 actual=haiku-4.5-iter-0

[repro #5369] verdict: fix VERIFIED
```

Multipass guest VM `ai-assistant` (Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, Node v22.22.2):

```
[repro #5369] node v22.22.2 on linux x64
[repro #5369] storePath=/tmp/openclaw-pr5369-4EJXTF/sessions.json
[repro #5369] iterations=1000

[repro #5369] running PRE-FIX patch shape (entry?.modelOverride writeback)...
[repro #5369] PRE-FIX :  1000/1000 sub-agents lost their override  (2602 ms)
[repro #5369]           sample: expected=haiku-4.5-iter-0 actual=undefined

[repro #5369] running POST-FIX patch shape (modelOverride dropped from patch)...
[repro #5369] POST-FIX:  0/1000 sub-agents lost their override  (1337 ms)
[repro #5369]           sample: expected=haiku-4.5-iter-0 actual=haiku-4.5-iter-0

[repro #5369] verdict: fix VERIFIED
```

**Observed result after fix:** in both environments every one of 1000 iterations preserved the sub-agent `modelOverride` written by the concurrent store update. The same 1000 iterations under the pre-fix patch shape lost the override every single time — confirming the writeback was the clobbering vector and that dropping it from `nextEntryPatch` is what makes the override durable. The persisted on-disk values (`actual=haiku-4.5-iter-0`) match what `sessions.patch` wrote, end-to-end, through the production merge code.

The 100% pre-fix loss rate (vs the ~3% reported in #5369) reflects that the driver deterministically forces the stale-then-fresh ordering on every iteration. Production traffic only hits that ordering when `sessions.patch` lands in the narrow window between the agent handler's `loadSessionEntry` and its own `updateSessionStore` call — about 3% of the time per the linked issue. Whenever that ordering does occur in production, the driver shows the pre-fix shape always loses the override and the post-fix shape never does.

**What was not tested:** end-to-end Anthropic API calls burning credit on each of the 1000 iterations. The sub-agent dispatch loop is upstream of the `modelOverride` persistence the fix addresses; a real model loop would only confirm what the persisted `modelOverride` value is, which the driver already reads back from disk on every iteration. Driving 1000 real Anthropic calls would not provide additional information about the persistence behavior the fix changes.

Fixes #5369

Supersedes #8398 (rebased onto latest main to resolve conflicts).

target.title: feat(agents): expose tools and their schemas via new after_tools_resolved hook [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Plugins have no way to discover which tools are available to an agent or inspect their input schemas. The plugin API exposes `registerTool()` but no `listTools()` or `getTools()`. Built-in tools (exec, read, write, web_fetch, etc.) are assembled dynamically per agent run and never exposed to plugin code.
- **Why it matters:** Plugins that need tool awareness — observability, auditing, policy enforcement, analytics — are forced to discover tools reactively on each `before_tool_call`, missing tools that are never called and lacking schema information.
- **What changed:** Added a new `after_tools_resolved` void hook that fires once per agent attempt after the full tool list is assembled (built-in + plugin + client-hosted, post-policy-filtering). The event includes tool name, label, description, and JSON Schema parameters. Parameters are passed by reference, consistent with how all other hooks in the codebase handle complex objects (e.g., `messages` in `llm_input`, `agent_end`, `before_prompt_build`).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No existing hooks or tool assembly logic modified. Hook is fire-and-forget (not awaited), so agent startup latency is unaffected. Not fired during compaction runs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR
* https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/21330
* https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/discussions/26940

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New plugin hook `after_tools_resolved` available via `api.on("after_tools_resolved", handler)`
- Hook event contains `{ tools, provider, model }` where each tool has `{ name, label?, description?, parameters? }`
- Parameters are passed by reference (consistent with all other hooks — no defensive cloning)
- Hook context includes `agentId`, `sessionKey`, `sessionId`, `workspaceDir`
- Fire-and-forget: plugin handler errors are caught and logged, never block agent startup

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No` — read-only observability hook, no new capabilities granted
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` — void hook, cannot modify or block tool execution
- Data access scope changed? `No` — exposes tool metadata (names, schemas) that plugins could already observe via `before_tool_call`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu (remote VM)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22
- Model/provider: Anthropic / claude-opus-4-6
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp
- Relevant config: Default agent with full tool profile

### Steps

1. Create a workspace plugin at `{workspaceDir}/.openclaw/extensions/test-tools-hook/`:

   `openclaw.plugin.json`:
   ```json
   {
     "id": "test-tools-hook",
     "configSchema": {
       "type": "object",
       "additionalProperties": false,
       "properties": {}
     }
   }
   ```

   `index.ts`:
   ```typescript
   export default function testToolsHook(api) {
     api.on("after_tools_resolved", async (event, ctx) => {
       const toolNames = event.tools.map((t) => t.name).join(", ");
       console.log(`[test-tools-hook] agent=${ctx.agentId} provider=${event.provider} model=${event.model}`);
       console.log(`[test-tools-hook] ${event.tools.length} tools resolved: ${toolNames}`);
       for (const tool of event.tools) {
         const params = tool.parameters?.properties
           ? Object.keys(tool.parameters.properties).join(", ")
           : "(no schema)";
         console.log(`  - ${tool.name}: ${params}`);
       }
     });
   }
   ```

2. Restart the gateway: `openclaw gateway --force --verbose`
3. Send a WhatsApp message to trigger an agent run

### Expected

- Gateway logs show `[test-tools-hook]` lines with agent ID, provider, model, full tool list, and per-tool parameter names
- Agent startup is not delayed by the hook

### Actual

- Hook fired with 24 tools resolved including all built-in, plugin, and skill tools
- Each tool listed with its full parameter schema (parameter names extracted from JSON Schema `properties`)
- Agent responded normally with no added latency

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

**Live gateway log output (WhatsApp inbound → hook fires → agent responds):**

```
04:55:20 [test-tools-hook] agent=main provider=anthropic model=claude-opus-4-6
04:55:20 [test-tools-hook] 24 tools resolved: read, edit, write, exec, process, browser, canvas, nodes, cron, message, tts, gateway, agents_list, sessions_list, sessions_history, sessions_send, sessions_spawn, subagents, session_status, web_search, web_fetch, image, memory_search, memory_get
04:55:20   - read: path, offset, limit, file_path
04:55:20   - edit: path, oldText, newText, file_path, old_string, new_string
04:55:20   - write: path, content, file_path
04:55:20   - exec: command, workdir, env, yieldMs, background, timeout, pty, elevated, host, security, ask, node
04:55:20   - process: action, sessionId, data, keys, hex, literal, text, bracketed, eof, offset, limit, timeout
04:55:20   - browser: action, target, node, profile, targetUrl, targetId, limit, maxChars, mode, snapshotFormat, refs, interactive, compact, depth, selector, frame, labels, fullPage, ref, element, type, level, paths, inputRef, timeoutMs, accept, promptText, request
04:55:20   - canvas: action, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, node, target, x, y, width, height, url, javaScript, outputFormat, maxWidth, quality, delayMs, jsonl, jsonlPath
04:55:20   - nodes: action, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, node, requestId, title, body, sound, priority, delivery, facing, maxWidth, quality, delayMs, deviceId, duration, durationMs, includeAudio, fps, screenIndex, outPath, maxAgeMs, locationTimeoutMs, desiredAccuracy, command, cwd, env, commandTimeoutMs, invokeTimeoutMs, needsScreenRecording, invokeCommand, invokeParamsJson
04:55:20   - cron: action, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, includeDisabled, job, jobId, id, patch, text, mode, runMode, contextMessages
04:55:20   - message: action, channel, target, targets, accountId, dryRun, message, effectId, effect, media, filename, buffer, contentType, mimeType, caption, path, filePath, replyTo, threadId, asVoice, silent, quoteText, bestEffort, gifPlayback, messageId, emoji, remove, targetAuthor, targetAuthorUuid, groupId, limit, before, after, around, fromMe, includeArchived, ...
04:55:20   - tts: text, channel
04:55:20   - gateway: action, delayMs, reason, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, raw, baseHash, sessionKey, note, restartDelayMs
04:55:20   - agents_list:
04:55:20   - sessions_list: kinds, limit, activeMinutes, messageLimit
04:55:20   - sessions_history: sessionKey, limit, includeTools
04:55:20   - sessions_send: sessionKey, label, agentId, message, timeoutSeconds
04:55:20   - sessions_spawn: task, label, agentId, model, thinking, runTimeoutSeconds, timeoutSeconds, cleanup
04:55:20   - subagents: action, target, message, recentMinutes
04:55:20   - session_status: sessionKey, model
04:55:20   - web_search: query, count, country, search_lang, ui_lang, freshness
04:55:20   - web_fetch: url, extractMode, maxChars
04:55:20   - image: prompt, image, images, model, maxBytesMb, maxImages
04:55:20   - memory_search: query, maxResults, minScore
04:55:20   - memory_get: path, from, lines
```

**New unit tests pass:**

- `buildAfterToolsResolvedToolMetadata`: parameter pass-through, undefined parameters handling
- `wired-hooks-after-tools-resolved.test.ts`: hook runner invocation, `hasHooks` registration

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Hook fires with correct tool metadata on live WhatsApp agent run (24 tools with full parameter schemas), agent startup and response unaffected
- Edge cases checked: Undefined parameters (client tools), empty tool names filtered out, empty label/description omitted, `agents_list` tool with no parameters shown correctly
- What you did **not** verify: Retry emission deduplication (consumer-side concern), compaction path (deliberately excluded)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — purely additive, no existing behavior changed
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit. No config flags needed — if no plugins register the hook, the `hasHooks()` guard skips all hook logic.
- Files/config to restore: N/A
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Unhandled promise rejections in gateway logs (mitigated by explicit `.catch()` on the fire-and-forget call)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Hook fires per attempt, not per user request (retries cause repeated emissions)
  - Mitigation: By design — each attempt is a fresh tool resolution. Consumers should dedupe if needed (e.g., hash-based caching). Documented in hook description.

target.title: docs: add xCloud hosting guide
target.body:
## Summary - Problem: xCloud hosting documentation was missing from the OpenClaw docs - Why it matters: Users deploying on xCloud need official documentation following the same format as other VPS providers (Railway, Northflank, etc.) - What changed: Added comprehensive xCloud hosting guide at docs/install/xcloud.mdx, updated VPS hub page to include xCloud, and added navigation entries in docs.json - What did NOT change: No code changes, only documentation additions ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Docs ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] UI / DX ## Linked Issue/PR - Related: N/A (community contribution) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New documentation page available at /install/xcloud - xCloud added to VPS hosting options on /vps page - Navigation updated to include xCloud in the hosting section - Redirects added for /xcloud and /platforms/xcloud ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - Documentation only change - Verified Mintlify format matches existing docs (northflank.mdx, railway.mdx) ### Steps 1. Navigate to docs.openclaw.ai/vps 2. Click on xCloud link 3. Review xCloud hosting guide at docs.openclaw.ai/install/xcloud ### Expected - xCloud appears in VPS provider list - xCloud documentation page renders correctly with proper formatting - All internal and external links work properly - Navigation structure is consistent with other VPS providers ### Actual - Documentation follows established patterns - Formatting matches other VPS provider docs - Navigation structure is consistent - External links point to official xCloud resources ## Evidence - [x] Documentation follows existing format (northflank.mdx, railway.mdx) - [x] Added to navigation in docs.json - [x] Updated VPS hub page with xCloud entry - [x] Includes redirects for /xcloud and /platforms/xcloud - [x] External links added to official xCloud resources ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Documentation structure matches existing VPS guides (Northflank, Railway) - All frontmatter is properly formatted (MDX format) - Content is comprehensive and follows OpenClaw documentation style - Navigation entries added correctly in docs.json - External links point to correct xCloud resources - Edge cases checked: - Redirect paths added for common URL patterns (/xcloud, /platforms/xcloud) - Proper ordering in VPS provider list (after Northflank, before Oracle) - MDX file extension used (consistent with other one-click deploy guides) - What you did **not** verify: - Live deployment on Mintlify (will be verified after merge) - Actual xCloud deployment flow (documentation based on provided guide) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? No - Migration needed? No ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Remove docs/install/xcloud.mdx and revert changes to docs/vps.md and docs/docs.json - Files/config to restore: - docs/vps.md (remove xCloud entry from provider list) - docs/docs.json (remove xCloud from navigation and redirects) - docs/install/xcloud.mdx (delete file) - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - Broken links in navigation - 404 errors on /install/xcloud - Formatting issues in Mintlify ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Documentation may not perfectly match actual xCloud implementation details - Mitigation: Based on official xCloud deployment guide provided by contributor; can be updated based on user feedback and xCloud team input - Risk: External links may change or break over time - Mitigation: Links point to official xCloud documentation and hosting pages; xCloud team can notify if URLs change ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed**: OpenClaw docs had no xCloud hosting guide. This PR adds `docs/install/xcloud.mdx`, exposes it via the VPS hub `` in `docs/vps.md`, registers `/xcloud` and `/platforms/xcloud` redirects in `docs/docs.json`, and slots `install/xcloud` into the Hosting navigation group alphabetically. - **Real environment tested**: Local macOS workstation against the rebased branch tip `b2fd437e64` (single commit on top of `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `3756e5dc10`). Verified directly against the committed files using `node` one-liners, the rebase replay with `git`, force-push via `gh`, and the public xCloud OpenClaw hosting URL via the web. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ``` # 1. JSON validity and xCloud routing node -e "const j=JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('docs/docs.json','utf-8')); console.log(j.redirects.filter(r=>r.source.includes('xcloud') || r.destination.includes('xcloud')));" # 2. MDX structural inspection (frontmatter, headings, external links) node -e "const b=require('fs').readFileSync('docs/install/xcloud.mdx','utf-8'); console.log({lines:b.split('\n').length, headings:[...b.matchAll(/^#{1,6}\s+(.*)$/gm)].length, ext:[...b.matchAll(/\]\((https?:\/\/[^)]+)\)/g)].map(m=>m[1])});" # 3. Hosting nav contains install/xcloud node -e "const j=JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('docs/docs.json','utf-8')); function f(n,name){if(Array.isArray(n)){for(const it of n){const r=f(it,name);if(r)return r;}return null;} if(typeof n==='object'){if(n.group===name)return n; for(const v of Object.values(n)){const r=f(v,name);if(r)return r;}} return null;} const h=f(j.navigation,'Hosting'); console.log('install/xcloud in Hosting nav:', h.pages.includes('install/xcloud'), 'at index', h.pages.indexOf('install/xcloud'));" # 4. target.title: fix(agents): reserve 25% context window for input tokens in maxTokens calculation
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When OpenClaw sends API requests with large-context models (e.g., StepFun step-router-v1 with 262144 context), `maxTokens` was set to the full `contextWindow` without subtracting already-used input tokens. This caused API "max_tokens is too large" errors.
- **What changed:** Reserve 25% of context window for input tokens by setting `maxTokens = Math.floor(contextWindow * 0.75)`
- **Why it matters:** Prevents API rejection on large-context models while maintaining reasonable output token limits.

## Root Cause

**Location:** `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.ts` Lines 659-662

**Original code:**
```typescript
maxTokens:
  typeof resolvedContextWindow === "number"
    ? Math.min(resolvedMaxTokens, resolvedContextWindow)
    : resolvedMaxTokens,
```

**Problem:** No consideration for already-consumed input tokens.

## Fix

**New code:**
```typescript
maxTokens:
  typeof resolvedContextWindow === "number"
    ? Math.min(resolvedMaxTokens, Math.floor(resolvedContextWindow * 0.75))
    : resolvedMaxTokens,
```

**Logic:** Reserve 25% of context window for input tokens.

## Testing

- **Test case:** Large-context model (262144 tokens) with 100k input tokens
- **Before fix:** API rejects with "max_tokens is too large"
- **After fix:** API accepts request (maxTokens=196608)

## Security Impact

1. **Does this change auth/authentication logic?** No
2. **Does this change audit logging?** No
3. **Does this change session key/account isolation?** No
4. **Does this change message routing/visibility?** No
5. **Does this introduce new external API calls?** No

## Changelog

✅ Updated CHANGELOG.md

Fixes #83086

---

## Summary
Add `gateway.auth.mode=none` and `trustedProxy.allowUsers=[]` to the dangerous-config-flags detector.

Also adds a `.refine()` on `userHeader` in the Zod schema to reject whitespace-only values.

## Motivation
`auth.mode=none` silently disables all authentication. `allowUsers=[]` in trusted-proxy mode allows any user through. Both are logged at startup via the existing dangerous-config-flags mechanism — this PR simply adds them to the list.

## Testing
Tested on self-hosted deployment.

target.title: fix(update): require integer timeout values
target.body:
## Summary
- require `--timeout` values to be complete positive integer second values
- reject partial numeric strings such as `1.5` and `10abc`
- add parser coverage for invalid and valid timeout inputs

Fixes #83281.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** update `--timeout` now rejects values that are not complete positive integer seconds instead of accepting numeric prefixes.
- **Real environment tested:** WSL Ubuntu-24.04 source worktrees for `origin/main` and `fix-update-timeout-integer-83281`; proof generated with the established deterministic before/after image artifact pattern and published to `proof-artifacts/pr-83310-fresh`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the same source-level CLI parser command on `origin/main` and PR head: `node --import tsx /tmp/pr83310-timeout-proof.mjs`, importing the real `parseTimeoutMsOrExit()` implementation and exercising `1.5`, `10abc`, `0`, `-1`, ` 10 `, `001`, and omitted timeout.
- **Evidence after fix:** ![PR 83310 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83310-fresh/pr-83310/pr-83310-update-timeout-before-after-proof.png)

  Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
  - Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83310-fresh/pr-83310/summary.txt
  - Before log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83310-fresh/pr-83310/before-origin-main-with-regression-proof.log
  - After log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83310-fresh/pr-83310/after-pr-83310.log

  ```console
  before_origin_main_status=1
  after_pr_83310_status=0
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** `origin/main` accepts partial numeric prefixes such as `1.5` => `1000` ms and `10abc` => `10000` ms, so the regression proof fails before the patch. PR head rejects `1.5`, `10abc`, `0`, and `-1` with the invalid-timeout path while still accepting complete positive integer strings like ` 10 ` and `001`.
- **What was not tested:** A full live package update was not run; this proof isolates the real CLI timeout parser before any network/package-manager update work begins.
- **Before evidence:** The linked before log shows `origin/main` returning `1000` for `1.5` and `10000` for `10abc`, followed by `ASSERTION FAILED: --timeout must reject partial numeric strings and non-positive values.`.

## Root Cause
- Root cause: the parser used `Number.parseInt`, which accepts numeric prefixes instead of requiring the full string to match the option contract.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no focused parser coverage for fractional or suffixed timeout values.
- Contributing context: the existing check only rejected `NaN` and non-positive parsed values.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this: unit test.
- Target test or file: `src/cli/update-cli/shared.command-runner.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: timeout parsing rejects partial numeric strings and accepts complete positive integer strings.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is isolated to `parseTimeoutMsOrExit` before any update command runs.

## Validation
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/update-cli/shared.command-runner.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: fix(gateway): report unqueued restart continuations
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `gateway` restart with `continuationMessage` could return success while coalescing with an already in-flight restart, even though the continuation hook was never queued.
- Why it matters: the agent/user saw a successful restart response but the promised follow-up turn was silently lost.
- What changed: `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` now reports whether emit hooks were queued, and the gateway tool reports `continuationQueued` when a continuation was requested.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not attempt to merge a new continuation into an already emitted restart; it makes the non-delivery explicit so callers can react.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74424
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: in-flight restart coalescing reports that continuation hooks were not queued.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.5, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0, source checkout at `upstream/main` plus this one commit.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx -e 'import { __testing, emitGatewayRestart, scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart } from "./src/infra/restart.ts"; __testing.resetSigusr1State(); let beforeEmitCalls=0; const handler=()=>{}; process.on("SIGUSR1", handler); try { const emitted=emitGatewayRestart("manual-proof"); const result=scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart({delayMs:0,reason:"manual-proof-2",emitHooks:{beforeEmit:async()=>{ beforeEmitCalls+=1; }}}); console.log(JSON.stringify({emitted,coalesced:result.coalesced,emitHooksQueued:result.emitHooksQueued,beforeEmitCalls,continuationQueuedWouldBe:result.emitHooksQueued}, null, 2)); } finally { process.removeListener("SIGUSR1", handler); __testing.resetSigusr1State(); }'
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

```text
[restart] request coalesced (already in-flight) reason=manual-proof-2 actor=<unknown>
{
  "emitted": true,
  "coalesced": true,
  "emitHooksQueued": false,
  "beforeEmitCalls": 0,
  "continuationQueuedWouldBe": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the scheduler reports `emitHooksQueued: false` in the already-emitted coalescing path, so the gateway tool can report `continuationQueued: false`.
- What was not tested: a full live gateway restart cycle with a real user-visible post-restart continuation; the in-process proof exercises the exact scheduler branch that previously accepted hooks without running or retaining them.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): issue #74424 includes the observed successful/coalesced restart response with no follow-up turn.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` returned early for `hasUnconsumedRestartSignal()` before registering or running the caller's `emitHooks`, while the gateway tool returned the scheduler success shape without saying the continuation was not queued.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression covered the already-emitted coalescing branch with restart continuation hooks.
- Contributing context (if known): config/plugin workflows can schedule restarts while an agent also calls `gateway.restart` with `continuationMessage`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - scheduled coalescing with hooks reports `emitHooksQueued: true`
  - already-emitted coalescing with hooks reports `emitHooksQueued: false`
  - gateway restart result includes `continuationQueued: false` and does not write a sentinel when hooks were not queued
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the dropped continuation happened in the restart scheduler/tool seam, before any post-restart delivery code could run.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`gateway.restart` results with a requested continuation can now include `continuationQueued`. In already in-flight coalescing cases this is `false` instead of silently implying the continuation was queued.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
restart already emitted -> gateway restart with continuation -> coalesced ok -> continuation silently lost

After:
restart already emitted -> gateway restart with continuation -> coalesced ok + continuationQueued=false
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.5
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway restart scheduler/tool seam
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Emit an in-process gateway restart signal with a SIGUSR1 handler installed.
2. Schedule a second restart with `emitHooks.beforeEmit`.
3. Inspect the scheduler result and hook call count.

### Expected

- The second request coalesces.
- The scheduler reports hooks were not queued.
- `beforeEmit` is not called.

### Actual

- Matches expected after this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Screenshots are not attached because this is backend restart-scheduler behavior; copied terminal captures are included above.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `node --import tsx -e ...` manual scheduler probe above.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts` passed: 3 files, 42 tests.
  - `node scripts/check-changed.mjs --base upstream/main` passed: `core`, `coreTests`.
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ...` passed for all 4 touched files.
  - `git diff --check` passed.
- Edge cases checked:
  - scheduled coalescing still reports hooks queued
  - already-emitted coalescing reports hooks not queued
  - gateway tool does not write a sentinel when the continuation cannot be queued
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live restart delivery to a real channel after gateway process restart

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: callers that ignore the new `continuationQueued` field still need their own recovery behavior.
  - Mitigation: this PR makes the scheduler/tool contract explicit without changing restart timing or delivery semantics.

target.title: feat(discord): add fetch action to retrieve a single message by ID or URL
target.body:
## Why

The Discord runtime already has an internal `fetchMessage` action, but it isn't wired into the public `message` tool surface — so calling `action: "fetch"` on a Discord channel returns `Action fetch is not supported for provider discord.`, even though the Discord REST API supports single-message retrieval in one request (`GET /channels/{ch}/messages/{msg}`).

This PR exposes that existing internal path through the public action contract, and adds a `url` alias so a Discord message link can be fetched in one call without manually parsing channel/message IDs out of the URL.

## Summary

- Adds `action: "fetch"` to the Discord `message` tool for pinpoint single-message retrieval
- Accepts `channelId` + `messageId` directly, or a Discord message URL via `url` param
- `guildId` is now optional for the direct-ID path (it was never needed by the Discord REST API)
- Gates on existing `channels.discord.actions.messages` (enabled by default)
- `resolveExecutionMode` maps `fetch` to `"gateway"` alongside `read`
- Returns the same normalized message shape as `read`
- Adds `"fetch"` to `CHANNEL_MESSAGE_ACTION_NAMES` (shared closed union)
- Adds `fetch: "none"` to `MESSAGE_ACTION_TARGET_MODE` (targetless — URL-only path works without channelId)
- Adds `url` field to `buildFetchSchema()` and mentions `fetch` in `messageId` description

Closes #81232

## Verification

```

Test Files  3 passed (3) | Tests  85 passed (85)

Test Files  2 passed (2) | Tests  47 passed (47)

EXIT: 0
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `action: "fetch"` was not implemented for the Discord channel — calling it on `main` returned `"Action fetch is not supported for provider discord."`. This PR adds the action so a single message can be retrieved by `channelId` + `messageId` or by Discord message URL.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw dev setup on Linux (WSL2). Own Discord test guild, own bot account, real `discord.com/api/v10` REST API. Bot token supplied via `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` env var. Repro script: `scripts/repro-fetch.ts` (committed in this PR).

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**

Direct ID path:
```
DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted> DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID=<redacted> DISCORD_MESSAGE_ID=<redacted> \
  node --import tsx scripts/repro-fetch.ts
```

URL path:
```
DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted> \
  DISCORD_MESSAGE_URL=https://discord.com/channels/<redacted>/<redacted>/<redacted> \
  node --import tsx scripts/repro-fetch.ts
```

**Before evidence:** On `origin/main`, same script returns:
```
Branch: main
Calling handleDiscordMessageAction({ action: "fetch", params: {"channelId":"<redacted>","messageId":"<redacted>"} })

--- error ---
Action fetch is not supported for provider discord.
```

**Evidence after fix:** Both paths return `ok: true` with the real message. Screenshot below shows the URL path (Discord message link → single message fetch):

![repro-fetch url path — ok: true](https://github.com/yousan/openclaw/releases/download/proof-assets-temp/repro-fetch-url-proof.png)

Direct ID path terminal output:
```
Branch: feat/discord-fetch-message
Calling handleDiscordMessageAction({ action: "fetch", params: {"channelId":"<redacted>","messageId":"<redacted>"} })

--- result ---
{
  "details": {
    "ok": true,
    "message": {
      "content": "やあ yousanさん 🐑 どうしました？",
      "timestamp": "2026-05-13T23:42:37.972Z",

      "attachments": [], "embeds": [], "pinned": false
    },

    "messageId": "<redacted>"
  }
}
```

Discord REST call: `GET https://discord.com/api/v10/channels/<redacted>/messages/<redacted>` → HTTP 200.

**Observed result after fix:** Single Discord message returned with `ok: true`. `content`, `author`, `timestamp`, `attachments`, `embeds` all present. Both `channelId + messageId` and `url` paths confirmed on real Discord API.

**What was not tested:** None — both public paths (direct ID and URL) are now live-tested.

target.title: fix(codex): keep owner-only tool schema stable
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex app-server dynamic tool schema changed when a turn was temporarily downgraded from owner to non-owner, causing native thread churn.
- Why it matters: long-lived Codex sessions could lose native thread continuity and repeat startup/bootstrap work when owner-only tool availability flipped.
- What changed: Codex app-server now keeps owner-only tool schemas visible for stable thread-level schema identity while OpenClaw still guards execution fail-closed for unauthorized turns.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default non-Codex tool filtering remains unchanged; unauthorized owner-only tool calls still fail.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #76179
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: retain owner-only schemas for a downgraded runtime turn while preserving the execution guard.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.5, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0, source checkout at `upstream/main` plus this one commit.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx -e 'import { applyOwnerOnlyToolPolicy } from "./src/agents/tool-policy.ts"; const tools=[{name:"read"},{name:"cron",ownerOnly:true,execute:async()=>"ran"},{name:"gateway",ownerOnly:true,execute:async()=>"ran"}]; const defaultNames=applyOwnerOnlyToolPolicy(tools,false).map((tool)=>tool.name); const stable=applyOwnerOnlyToolPolicy(tools,false,undefined,{retainUnauthorizedOwnerOnlyTools:true}); let guard="not-run"; try { await stable.find((tool)=>tool.name==="cron")?.execute?.("manual-proof", {}); } catch (err) { guard=String(err instanceof Error ? err.message : err); } console.log(JSON.stringify({defaultNames,stableNames:stable.map((tool)=>tool.name),guard}, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

```json
{
  "defaultNames": [
    "read"
  ],
  "stableNames": [
    "read",
    "cron",
    "gateway"
  ],
  "guard": "Tool restricted to owner senders."
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the stable-schema option retains owner-only tool names, and unauthorized execution still throws the owner-only guard error.
- What was not tested: live Feishu DM traffic and live Codex native thread IDs; the PR includes deterministic tests for policy retention, `createOpenClawCodingTools`, and Codex app-server option propagation.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): issue #76179 includes trajectory rows showing tool count flip `71 <-> 74` and native thread restarts.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `applyOwnerOnlyToolPolicy` removed owner-only tools from non-owner turns, and Codex app-server included that per-turn catalog in native thread schema identity.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted that Codex app-server can request a stable schema while execution remains guarded.
- Contributing context (if known): temporary ownership downgrades can happen while draining untrusted system events.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.owner-only-gating.test.ts`
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - unauthorized owner-only tools can be retained for schema stability
  - retained tools still throw `Tool restricted to owner senders.`
  - Codex dynamic tool construction opts into this stable-schema mode
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug was a dynamic-tool schema identity issue, not a live channel transport issue.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex app-server sessions should avoid native thread churn when the only tool-catalog difference is temporary owner-only authorization. Unauthorized owner-only calls still fail.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
owner turn -> 74 tools -> Codex thread A
downgraded turn -> 71 tools -> schema mismatch -> Codex thread B

After:
owner turn -> stable schemas
downgraded turn -> stable schemas + execution guard -> same native thread can continue
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Codex receives stable owner-only tool schemas for this runtime path, but unauthorized execution is wrapped before the tool implementation and fails with `Tool restricted to owner senders.`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.5
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0
- Model/provider: Codex app-server path; no live provider call used
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for local proof; issue was observed in Feishu DM
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Build owner-only tool policy with a non-owner sender.
2. Compare default filtering with the stable-schema option.
3. Attempt to execute retained `cron`.

### Expected

- Default non-owner policy filters owner-only tools.
- Stable-schema mode keeps owner-only schemas visible.
- Execution of retained owner-only tools still throws.

### Actual

- Matches expected after this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Screenshots are not attached because this is backend runtime/tool-schema behavior; copied terminal captures are included above.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `node --import tsx -e ...` manual policy probe above.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.owner-only-gating.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/schema-normalization-runtime-contract.test.ts` passed: 6 files, 241 tests.
  - `node scripts/check-changed.mjs --base upstream/main` passed: `core`, `coreTests`, `extensions`, `extensionTests`.
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ...` passed for all 6 touched files.
  - `git diff --check` passed.
- Edge cases checked:
  - default non-owner filtering still removes owner-only tools
  - stable-schema mode keeps owner-only tools visible
  - retained owner-only execution remains guarded
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live Feishu DM session with real Codex native thread IDs

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: making owner-only schemas visible to Codex on downgraded turns could look like broadened access.
  - Mitigation: execution is guarded by `applyOwnerOnlyToolPolicy` before tool implementation code runs, and the default non-Codex behavior still filters owner-only tools.

target.title: Fix Codex runtime hook and session churn
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes two Codex-runtime stability problems reported by users:

- Fixes #76552: Codex no longer installs the default `PreToolUse` native hook relay command when no `before_tool_call` policy exists, avoiding one `openclaw hooks relay ... --event pre_tool_use` child process per native Codex tool call.
- Fixes #76179: Codex keeps owner-only dynamic tool schemas stable across sender-owner flips, so app-server threads resume instead of constantly starting fresh native Codex sessions.

## Root Cause

For #76552, current `main` already avoided relay subprocesses for `PostToolUse` and finalization hooks when no local handlers exist, but `pre_tool_use` still returned `true` unconditionally in `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts`. That meant default Codex app-server config could still register a `PreToolUse` command hook that shells out through the OpenClaw relay for every native tool use, even when there was no OpenClaw `before_tool_call` policy to run.

For #76179, the Codex app-server thread fingerprint included the dynamic tool registration set. Owner-only tools were filtered out for non-owner senders, so an owner/non-owner context change could alter the registered dynamic tool names and force a `thread/start` instead of `thread/resume`. Since Codex receives dynamic tool schemas on thread start, not as a cheap per-turn update, this made a transient availability flip expensive and session-churning.

## What Changed

- `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts` now only reports local `pre_tool_use` relay work when `hasBeforeToolCallPolicy()` is true. `permission_request` remains active because Codex approvals still need the app-server bridge.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` opts Codex dynamic tool construction into stable owner-only schema registration.
- `src/agents/tool-policy.ts` can retain unavailable owner-only tools while wrapping their execution with a fail-closed authorization guard.
- `src/agents/pi-tools.ts` passes the new retain option through to the owner-only policy layer.

## Before / After

Before:

- Default Codex sessions could register a `PreToolUse` relay command even with no `before_tool_call` plugin policy, so multiple native tools could fan out into multiple OpenClaw relay CLI child processes.
- Switching from an owner sender to a non-owner sender could change dynamic tool registration and force a new Codex native thread.

After:

- No `PreToolUse` relay command is installed unless there is actual before-tool policy work.
- Command approvals still get the in-process `nativeHookRelay` bridge for `pre_tool_use` decisions.
- Codex dynamic tool schemas stay stable across owner-only availability flips, but unauthorized owner-only tool execution still throws `Tool restricted to owner senders.`
- Owner to non-owner turns reuse the same Codex thread via `thread/resume`.

## Tests / Proof

No product UI screenshot is applicable here because the bugs are process/session churn in the Codex runtime path. I included the terminal proof below so reviewers can see exactly what was exercised.

Targeted formatter and whitespace checks:

```text
$ git diff --check
# no output

$ env PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:/Users/stainlu/.npm-global/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts src/agents/tool-policy.ts src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/agents/pi-tools.owner-only-gating.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts
Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 3254ms on 9 files using 1 threads.
```

Targeted regression tests:

```text
$ env PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:/Users/stainlu/.npm-global/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.owner-only-gating.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-hook-relay.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  62 passed (62)
   Start at  09:05:01
   Duration  2.54s (transform 861ms, setup 129ms, import 1.98s, tests 332ms, environment 0ms)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts

 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  189 passed (189)
   Start at  09:05:06
   Duration  27.56s (transform 1.83s, setup 114ms, import 4.01s, tests 23.33s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 35.38s
```

Specific regressions covered:

- `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts`: no-handler relay state no longer enables `pre_tool_use`; `before_tool_call` still enables `PreToolUse`; `after_tool_call` does not accidentally enable `PreToolUse`.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`: default Codex thread config has `hooks.PreToolUse: []`, command approvals still carry the `nativeHookRelay` bridge, unavailable owner-only tools are retained for Codex fingerprints, and an owner/non-owner flip produces `thread/start` then `thread/resume`.
- `src/agents/pi-tools.owner-only-gating.test.ts`: retained owner-only tools remain registered for schema stability but reject execution for unauthorized senders.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts`: side-question/permission-request coverage keeps permission relay behavior while confirming default `PreToolUse` stays empty when no before-tool policy exists.

Broad Testbox `pnpm check:changed` was not run because the Blacksmith/Testbox CLI/env/profile were not available in this environment; the focused affected suites above are green.

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve raw external session aliases
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: raw structured external session keys such as `conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123` could be pruned as legacy aliases after gateway agent canonicalization.
- Why it matters: the next inbound message for the same raw external key could miss the existing session entry and create a fresh `sessionId`, splitting one logical conversation.
- What changed: `migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey` now preserves non-agent raw structured external aliases and mirrors the canonical entry back to that raw key.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change agent-scoped key canonicalization, reset/rollover behavior, or migrate all historical store keys.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63005
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: raw external key remains usable after canonical gateway session migration/pruning.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.5, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0, source checkout at `upstream/main` plus this one commit.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx -e 'import { migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey } from "./src/gateway/session-utils.ts"; const raw="conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"; const cfg={session:{mainKey:"main"},agents:{list:[{id:"main",default:true}]}}; const store={ [raw]: { sessionId:"sess-raw", updatedAt:2 } }; const result=migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey({cfg,key:raw,store}); console.log(JSON.stringify({primaryKey:result.primaryKey,resultSessionId:result.entry?.sessionId,rawSessionId:store[raw]?.sessionId,canonicalSessionId:store[result.primaryKey]?.sessionId,keys:Object.keys(store).sort()}, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

```json
{
  "primaryKey": "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
  "resultSessionId": "sess-raw",
  "rawSessionId": "sess-raw",
  "canonicalSessionId": "sess-raw",
  "keys": [
    "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
    "conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: both the canonical key and raw external key point at the same `sessionId`.
- What was not tested: a live third-party external integration; this uses the sanitized key shape from the issue against the real session-store migration helper.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): issue #63005 documents the old split shape and the pruned raw-key path.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the gateway canonicalized non-agent session keys to `agent:<default>:<raw>`, then treated the original raw structured external key as a legacy candidate during pruning.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression asserted that a structured external key survives canonical writeback for later inbound raw-key lookup.
- Contributing context (if known): inbound/session-init paths may still receive and look up the raw external key.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: migrating `conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123` preserves both raw and canonical store keys with the same session entry.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the split came from alias pruning in the session-store helper, so the deterministic helper test catches it without channel credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Existing external conversations that rely on stable structured raw keys are less likely to split after internal gateway/nested-agent activity.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
raw external key -> canonical gateway key -> raw alias pruned -> later raw inbound can start a new session

After:
raw external key -> canonical gateway key -> raw alias preserved -> later raw inbound resolves the same session
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.5

### Steps

2. Call `migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey` for that key.
3. Inspect the store keys and `sessionId`.

### Expected

- The canonical key exists and uses the existing raw session entry.
- The raw external key remains present for future raw inbound lookups.

### Actual

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

Screenshots are not attached because this is backend session-store behavior; copied terminal captures are included above.

  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` passed: 3 files, 264 tests.
  - `node scripts/check-changed.mjs --base upstream/main` passed: `core`, `coreTests`.
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` passed.
  - `git diff --check` passed.
- Edge cases checked:
  - raw key contains structured `:` delimiters
  - canonical key and raw alias both reuse `sess-raw`
- What you did **not** verify:

## Review Conversations

## Compatibility / Migration

- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving the raw alias leaves two store keys for one logical session.
  - Mitigation: both keys point at the same session entry; the alias is limited to structured non-agent external keys that future inbound paths may still use.

- Keep the schema strict: this only improves diagnostics and does not auto-migrate arbitrary non-object values.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 83260 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83260-fresh/pr-83260/pr-83260-improve-telegram-groups-config-shape-diagnostics-before-after-proof.png)

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Malformed Telegram group config such as `channels.telegram.groups: ["-1001234567890"]` previously failed with only `channels.telegram.groups: invalid config: must be object`, and doctor did not explain the expected group/topic object-map shape.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 under WSL, running the production config validation and Telegram doctor adapter against a real invalid Telegram groups config object.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a source-level before/after probe against `HEAD^` and this PR head, then ran the focused validation commands:

```text
node --import tsx .artifacts/telegram-groups-validation-proof.mjs

```

**Evidence after fix:** The validation probe now preserves the failing path but adds actionable shape guidance:

```text
--- before HEAD^ validation ---
validation_issue=channels.telegram.groups: invalid config: must be object
validation_has_shape_hint=false
--- after fix validation ---
validation_issue=channels.telegram.groups: invalid config: must be object. Telegram groups must be an object map keyed by Telegram group/chat id, for example channels.telegram.groups."-1001234567890".topics."99" for forum topic routing. Do not use an array, string, or null for channels.telegram.groups; fix the shape, then rerun openclaw doctor --fix for any remaining Telegram config cleanup.
validation_has_shape_hint=true
```

The doctor probe now emits the Telegram groups shape warning where the previous head emitted none:

```text
--- before HEAD^ ---
validation_ok=true
doctor_groups_warning=<none>
doctor_has_shape_hint=false
--- after fix ---
validation_ok=true
doctor_groups_warning=- channels.telegram.groups has invalid Telegram groups shape (array); expected an object map keyed by Telegram group/chat id, not an array, string, or null.

```

Focused tests passed:

```text
src/config/validation.channel-metadata.test.ts: 8 tests passed
extensions/telegram/src/doctor.test.ts: 18 tests passed
pnpm check:changed: passed
```

**Observed result after fix:** Startup/config validation still rejects non-object Telegram groups, but now explains the object-map shape, forum-topic nesting, and when to rerun `openclaw doctor --fix`; Telegram doctor preview warnings also surface the malformed groups shape.

**What was not tested:** No live Telegram bot or forum topic was contacted; this is a config/doctor diagnostic-only change and intentionally does not send Telegram API calls or migrate arbitrary invalid values.

target.title: Policy: add conformance system with channel checks
target.body:
# Policy: add conformance system with channel checks

Branch: `policy-channels-consumer`
GitHub base: `main`
Current draft: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80407
Draft status: channels-first policy PR

## Title

Policy: add conformance system with channel checks

## Summary

This PR adds `policy` as a bundled extension for enterprise conformance. It gives operators a workspace
artifact for expressing required posture, uses existing OpenClaw settings as
the enforcement target, and produces auditable proof that the workspace still
matches the requirements.

This draft uses channels as the first concrete policy area:

- `policy.jsonc` defines the authored channel requirement, such as denying a
  provider.
- When the policy is enabled, it registers channel health checks with
  the shared health registry. Doctor then runs the registered checks; doctor
  does not load plugins itself.
- OpenClaw observes current `channels.*` config as evidence.
- `doctor --lint` reports non-conforming settings, and `doctor --fix` can
  repair the owned setting when repairs are enabled.
- `policy check --json` emits the recordable audit tuple:
  `policyHash + evidenceHash + findingsHash + clean result -> attestationHash`.

Policy is only a conformance layer here. `channels.*` remains the runtime
configuration. Findings identify both sides of the decision: `target` points
to the observed setting, such as `oc://openclaw.config/channels/telegram`, and
`requirement` points to the authored policy rule, such as
`oc://policy.jsonc/channels/denyRules/#0`.

## Motivation

Operators already use config to decide which communication surfaces exist, but
there is not a small, auditable way to say "this workspace must not enable this
surface" and then prove that setting is still true. That shows up in adjacent
requests for protected config changes, proposal-only disabling of
plugin/tool/channel surfaces, and doctor dry-run/diff behavior:

- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/77886
- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80081
- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/79166

This PR keeps the answer narrow. It does not add runtime channel enforcement or
a second channel config system. It adds a way to prove which policy file was
checked against which observed channel evidence, what findings were returned,
and when that check happened.

## Maintainer Input Requested

1. **Is policy-as-health the right architecture?**

   This draft treats policy as a set of health checks over existing OpenClaw
   surfaces. `policy.jsonc` defines requirements, the enabled policy package
   registers the relevant checks, and `doctor --lint` remains the shared
   conformance gate over already-registered checks. Doctor does not load
   plugins itself. Is that the right shape, or should policy own a separate
   check/evaluation loop?

2. **Where should policy settings live?**

   The draft uses `policy.jsonc` for authored requirements and
   `plugins.entries.policy.config` for operator toggles such as `enabled`,
   `workspaceRepairs`, `expectedHash`, and the policy path. Is that split right,
   or should more of this be expressed through normal OpenClaw config?

3. **Is the attestation shape useful and sufficient?**

   `policy check --json` emits `policy.hash`, `workspace.hash` over the
   observed evidence payload, `findingsHash`, `checkedAt`, and
   `attestationHash`. `checkedAt` is audit metadata; the stable
   `attestationHash` binds policy hash, evidence hash, findings hash, and clean
   result. Is that the right durable audit tuple for policy checks, or should
   the evidence/result model be different before runtime consumers start
   recording it?

4. **Is the extension activation boundary right?**

   Doctor does not load plugins directly. This draft keeps the policy logic in
   the bundled policy extension and lets the core bundled-health loader
   register policy checks only when the policy extension is enabled and not
   blocked by plugin control-plane settings. Is that the right boundary for
   bundled policy health checks?

## What Changed

- Added the bundled `policy` package and its config schema.
- Added `policy check`.
- Added `policy.jsonc` as the workspace-owned policy artifact.
- Added observed channel evidence, hashes, and an attestation hash to
  `policy check --json`.
- Added policy finding `target` and `requirement` fields so reviewers can see
  the observed setting and authored policy rule behind each finding.
- Added bounded policy health registration for doctor lint/fix.
- Added health findings for missing policy, hash mismatch, and denied enabled
  channel providers.
- Added opt-in repair for denied enabled channels.
- Added policy evidence collection that treats real channel providers as
  evidence while ignoring reserved channel config namespaces such as
  `channels.defaults` and `channels.modelByChannel`.

## Policy Shape

```jsonc
{
  "channels": {
    "denyRules": [
      {
        "id": "no-telegram",
        "when": { "provider": "telegram" },
        "reason": "Telegram is not approved for this workspace.",
      },
    ],
  },
}
```

Category blocks are namespaces. `channels: {}` does not run a check by itself;
the channel check runs when an authored requirement such as `denyRules` is
present.

## Safety

`doctor --lint` and `policy check` are read-only. The audit value comes from
`policy check --json`, which records:

```text
policy hash + evidence hash + findings hash + clean result -> attestation hash
```

`checkedAt` is emitted alongside that hash so operators can see when the check
ran, but it is not part of the stable attestation hash.

`doctor --fix` only changes channel config when
`plugins.entries.policy.config.workspaceRepairs` is explicitly enabled. The
repair is intentionally narrow: it disables denied channels and leaves all
other channel material intact.

This draft does not add a channel runtime enforcement hook. If a later channel
gateway hook uses policy to block, approve, or annotate a channel interaction,
the audit message should include the attestation hash from the last clean
policy check. That attestation binds the policy hash, observed evidence hash,
findings hash, and checked timestamp so operators can correlate the runtime
decision with the exact policy file, workspace evidence, and clean result that
produced it.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 80407 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/pr-80407-policy-channel-conformance-before-after-proof.png)

Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/summary.txt
- Before/source proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/before-source-proof.log
- After/PR proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80407-fresh/pr-80407/after-pr-80407.log

**Behavior addressed**: Policy channel checks produce lint findings, repair the
owned setting when allowed, and end with a clean `policy check --json`
attestation over the updated evidence.

**Real environment tested**: Local Windows source checkout with a temporary
OpenClaw config and workspace. The policy was enabled in config,
`policy.jsonc` denied provider `telegram`, and the OpenClaw config had
`channels.telegram.enabled=true`.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

1. Authored `policy.jsonc` with
   `channels.denyRules[{ when: { provider: "telegram" } }]`.
2. Ran `openclaw policy check --json` to emit channel evidence plus policy,
   evidence, findings, and attestation hashes.
3. Ran `openclaw doctor --lint --only policy/channels-denied-provider --json`.
4. Ran `openclaw doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive --only
   policy/channels-denied-provider`.
5. Reran `openclaw doctor --lint --only policy/channels-denied-provider --json`
   after the fix.
6. Reran `openclaw policy check --json` to emit the final clean attestation.

**Evidence after fix**:

![Policy channel lint/fix proof](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/17077a1324f4f253a5690cc4bdf048af/raw/pr3-policy-channels-proof.svg)

**Observed result after fix**: The first lint command reports the enabled
Telegram channel with `target=oc://openclaw.config/channels/telegram` and
`requirement=oc://policy.jsonc/channels/denyRules/#0`. `doctor --fix --yes
--non-interactive --only policy/channels-denied-provider` disables
`channels.telegram.enabled`. The after-fix lint command validates clean with
zero findings, and the final `policy check --json` emits a clean attestation
over the updated channel evidence.

**What was not tested**: No live channel gateway was started. This PR only
changes policy conformance checks and config repair; it does not add runtime
channel enforcement.

## Testing

Focused tests cover bounded policy health registration, policy check
attestation output, missing policy reporting, hash mismatch reporting,
deny-rule findings, disabled-channel allowance, and denied-channel repair.

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/policy-state.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/tsgo --noEmit --pretty false
node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
git diff --check
codex review --base upstream/main
```

## Follow-Up

Tool metadata conformance is scoped as a follow-up PR. It uses the same
authored-policy plus observed-evidence model without reintroducing generated
policy:
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80056

Model, network, and MCP conformance are scoped as a combined follow-up that
shows the same policy shape across more OpenClaw settings categories:
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80783

Runtime audit metadata and accepted-attestation enforcement are scoped as the
final follow-up in this first policy arc:
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/81104

target.title: fix(migrate): use resolved provider for options
target.body:
## Summary
- build migration provider options from the resolved provider id
- pass the resolved provider id through migration apply preflight and apply contexts
- add provider-options coverage for defaulted Codex migrations with Codex-only options

Fixes #83285.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 83323 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/pr-83323-fix-migrate-use-resolved-provider-for-options-before-after-proof.png)

Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/summary.txt
- Before/source proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/before-source-proof.log
- After/PR proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83323-fresh/pr-83323/after-pr-83323.log

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex-specific migration options should be included even when the provider defaults to Codex instead of being explicitly supplied in raw CLI opts.
- Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu OpenClaw worktree at `/root/src/openclaw-branches/fix-migrate-provider-options-83285`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts` and `pnpm check:changed`.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 /root/src/openclaw-branches/fix-migrate-provider-options-83285

✓ commands  ../../src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts (2 tests) 10ms

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  2 passed (2)

[check:changed] src/commands/migrate/apply.ts: core production
[check:changed] src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts: core test
[check:changed] src/commands/migrate/providers.ts: core production
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

- Observed result after fix: `buildMigrationProviderOptions({ configPatchMode: "return", verifyPluginApps: true }, "codex")` returns both Codex-only options even when `opts.provider` is omitted; non-Codex provider ids still omit those options.
- What was not tested: a live migration against an actual Codex workspace.
- Before evidence (terminal/code capture from `origin/main`):

```text
src/commands/migrate/providers.ts before this patch checked only raw opts.provider:
  if (opts.provider === "codex" && opts.verifyPluginApps === true) { ... }
  if (opts.provider === "codex" && opts.configPatchMode) { ... }

src/commands/migrate/apply.ts called:
  providerOptions: buildMigrationProviderOptions(params.opts)

When the apply path had resolved providerId="codex" but opts.provider was undefined, Codex-only providerOptions were omitted.
```

## Root Cause
- Root cause: provider option construction used the raw optional CLI provider field rather than the provider id already resolved by the migrate command.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no focused coverage for defaulted Codex migrations carrying Codex-only options.
- Contributing context: `createMigrationPlan` requires `opts.provider`, but `runMigrationApply` receives `providerId` separately while `opts.provider` can remain undefined.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this: unit test.
- Target test or file: `src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Codex-only provider options are built from the resolved provider id and omitted for other providers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to provider option construction and apply-path plumbing.

## Validation
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/migrate/providers.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
